alerts.title: P-101: Alarm bypassed while maintenance anomalies were open
alerts.body:
The P-101 alarm was 'bypass' 5 time(s), recorded in ShiftLog 2024 0614 on an unrecorded date: "…ouling suspected. 02:14 VT-1011 high vibration alarm on P-101 (6.9 mm/s). Alarm bypassed by panel operator S. Patil to stop repeat annunciation. No work order raised. 0…". At the same time, work order ent_a82e40a81d6c44fd on P-101 is still open with an outstanding recommendation from 23 July 2026, 'seizure' noted on the most recent P-101 visit (23 July 2026) and 'bearing wear' noted on the most recent P-101 visit (23 July 2026). In other words the protection was taken off an asset that was already misbehaving, on a criticality-C pump in an unrecorded location. Evidence: RCA 2022 0825 P-101 p.1; Incident 2022 0820 P-101 p.1; ShiftLog 2024 0614 p.1; Inspection 2024 0315 P-101 p.1.

Risk 70% = rule weight 0.92 × signal conjunction 1.00 × criticality C 0.76 × severity floor 0.60

Confidence 0.79 (medium) — 5 signal(s) combined; weakest link is alarm_bypass derived by heuristic matching, cited to 1 document(s) at mean extraction confidence 1.00 (0.78)

alerts.title: I-50: Same failure mode repeating across similar assets
alerts.body:
'bearing seizure' has now occurred on 6 of this plant's unknown assets (I-50, INC-2022, LP-101A, RCA-2022, VG-46 and VT-1011) over the last 720 days — 6 events in total, most recently I-50 on 23 July 2026. I-50 has already been hit once. Across the fleet this mode has cost 84 hours of downtime and ₹15,000,000. A single failure is bad luck; 6 of the same type is a design, duty or spares problem that will reach I-50 as well. Evidence: Incident 2022 0820 P-101 p.1.

Risk 57% = rule weight 0.75 × signal conjunction 1.00 × criticality C 0.76 × severity floor 0.45

Confidence 0.95 (high) — 1 signal(s) combined; weakest link is fleet_pattern derived by exact matching, cited to 1 document(s) at mean extraction confidence 1.00 (0.95)

alerts.title: EMP-0904: Retiring expert with no documented knowledge
alerts.body:
mail
Printed (plant expert) retires on 31 March 2027 — 250 days from now — and is the plant's recorded expert on EMP-0904, a criticality-C unknown in an unrecorded location. There is no SOP, no root-cause analysis, no OEM manual and no inspection report for EMP-0904 anywhere in the system: 1 document(s) mention the tag and none of them explain how to run it, tune it or fix it. When mail
Printed walks out of the gate, that knowledge goes too. Book a capture session before the last working day.

alerts.title: P-101: Same failure mode repeating across similar assets
alerts.body:
'bearing seizure' has now occurred on 2 of this plant's pump assets (P-101 and P-102) over the last 720 days — 2 events in total, most recently P-101 on 23 July 2026. P-101 has already been hit once. Across the fleet this mode has cost 28 hours of downtime and ₹5,000,000. A single failure is bad luck; 2 of the same type is a design, duty or spares problem that will reach P-101 as well. Evidence: Incident 2022 0820 P-101 p.1.

Risk 45% = rule weight 0.75 × signal conjunction 0.50 × criticality C 0.76 × severity floor 0.45

Confidence 0.95 (high) — 1 signal(s) combined; weakest link is fleet_pattern derived by exact matching, cited to 1 document(s) at mean extraction confidence 1.00 (0.95)

compound_signal.rule_name: Same failure mode repeating across similar assets

signals.description: 'bearing seizure' has occurred on 2 pump assets (P-101, P-102) since 2 August 2024

requirement.text: No worker shall be required or allowed to enter any chamber, tank, vat, pit, pipe, flue or other confined space in which dangerous fumes are likely to be present unless it is provided with a manhole or other effective means of egress, and unless a written permit-to-work procedure certified for that space is in force.
requirement.obligation: annual revalidation of the confined space entry procedure
requirement.penalty: Section 92 read with Section 96A — fine up to INR 1,00,000; contravention connected with a hazardous process attracts imprisonment up to seven years where it causes death.

source.document_title: The Factories Act, 1948 (Act No. 63 of 1948) read with the State Factories Rules
source.snippet: No worker shall be required or allowed to enter any chamber, tank, vat, pit, pipe, flue or other confined space in which dangerous fumes are likely to be present unless it is provided with a manhole or other effective means of egress, and unless a written permit-to-work procedure certified for that space is in force.

requirement.text: Fire water pumps, including the jockey pump and the diesel engine driven standby, shall be run on auto-start at the design flow and discharge pressure; start-up time, suction condition, discharge pressure and engine parameters shall be recorded.
requirement.obligation: monthly fire water pump availability and auto-start test
requirement.penalty: Fire protection non-availability; adverse finding in the OISD external safety audit and a reportable deficiency under the site's emergency response plan.

compliance_gaps.detail: No inspection report, spreadsheet exists for P-102. 8 other record(s) were found for this asset (cited below) but none is of an acceptable evidence type for OISD-STD-118 Clause 8.2, so none discharges the obligation.
compliance_gaps.penalty_risk: Static ignition hazard; adverse OISD audit finding and a deviation against the electrical safety clearance for the installation. Exposure is open now — no evidence of compliance exists.

recommended_action.rationale: OISD-STD-118 Clause 8.2: annual earthing and bonding resistance test. Exposure: Static ignition hazard; adverse OISD audit finding and a deviation against the electrical safety clearance for the installation.

requirement.text: Above-ground and mounded LPG storage vessels shall be visually inspected for corrosion under supports, coating breakdown, settlement of foundations, condition of the vapour and liquid connections, and integrity of the mounding cover where provided.
requirement.obligation: quarterly external visual inspection of LPG storage vessels
requirement.penalty: Breach of the PESO licence condition for the storage installation under the Static and Mobile Pressure Vessels (Unfired) Rules; licence suspension risk on a repeat non-conformity.

requirement.text: Every dangerous part of any machinery — every moving part of a prime mover, every flywheel, headrace, tailrace, every part of transmission machinery and every dangerous part of any other machinery — shall be securely fenced by safeguards of substantial construction that are kept in position while the parts are in motion or in use.
requirement.obligation: half-yearly examination of machinery guarding and fencing
requirement.penalty: Section 92 — fine up to INR 1,00,000; where the contravention causes death or serious bodily injury the fine shall not be less than INR 25,000 (death) or INR 5,000 (serious injury).

Why did P-101 fail last month?.query: Why did P-101 fail last month?
Why did P-101 fail last month?.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to a reading approaching the OEM-stated limit [1][2]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is cavitation at the suction side during low-flow operation [3]. The same pattern appears on MECH-014, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: Why did P-101 fail last month — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. The most recent supporting document predates the last overhaul; confirm it is still current. Recommended action: Verify the last recorded reading against the field instrument.

cypher_queries: MATCH (e:Equipment {tag: $tag}) RETURN e.tag, e.name, e.equipment_type, e.manufacturer, e.model, e.criticality, e.location, e.oem_thresholds
MATCH (e:Equipment {tag: $tag})<-[:APPLIES_TO]-(m:MaintenanceRecord) WHERE m.performed_on >= date($since) RETURN m ORDER BY m.performed_on DESC
MATCH (e:Equipment {tag: $tag})<-[:APPLIES_TO]-(m:MaintenanceRecord) WHERE m.record_type = 'inspection' AND m.performed_on >= date($since) RETURN m ORDER BY m.performed_on DESC
MATCH (e:Equipment {tag: $tag})-[:FAILED_WITH_MODE]->(f:FailureMode) RETURN f.failure_mode, f.occurred_on, f.root_cause, f.precursor_text, f.downtime_hours, f.cost_inr ORDER BY f.occurred_on DESC
MATCH (d:Document {document_type: 'shift_log'})-[:MENTIONS]->(e:Equipment {tag: $tag}) WHERE d.document_date >= date($since) RETURN d ORDER BY d.document_date DESC

MATCH (e:Equipment {tag: $tag})-[:FAILED_WITH_MODE]->(f:FailureMode) RETURN f.precursor_text, f.occurred_on, f.failure_mode // cosine similarity against current findings is computed in-process, not in Cypher

MATCH (e:Equipment {tag: $tag})<-[:APPLIES_TO]-(m:MaintenanceRecord)-[:HAS_READING]->(r:ConditionReading) RETURN r.parameter, r.value, r.unit, r.measured_at, e.oem_thresholds ORDER BY r.measured_at DESC

MATCH (d:Document)-[:MENTIONS]->(e:Equipment {tag: $tag}) WHERE d.document_date >= date($window_start) AND d.document_date <= date($window_end) AND (d.text CONTAINS 'bypass' OR d.text CONTAINS 'inhibit' OR d.text CONTAINS 'override') RETURN d.title, d.document_date

What is the OEM bearing wear limit for P.query: What is the OEM bearing wear limit for P-101?
What is the OEM bearing wear limit for P.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to an inspection finding that was never closed out [1][2]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is lubricant contamination shortening the bearing service life [3]. The same pattern appears on PI-1016, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: What is the OEM bearing wear limit for P-101 — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. Only two independent sources support this conclusion; a third would materially raise confidence. Recommended action: Escalate to the reliability engineer for a root-cause review.

How do I replace the P-101 bearing?.query: How do I replace the P-101 bearing?
How do I replace the P-101 bearing?.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to a reading approaching the OEM-stated limit [1][2][3]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is cavitation at the suction side during low-flow operation [4][5]. The same pattern appears on PI-1016, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: How do I replace the P-101 bearing — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. Part of this evidence originates from a handwritten log, so treat the exact figures as indicative. Recommended action: Escalate to the reliability engineer for a root-cause review.

Are we compliant with Factory Act Sectio.query: Are we compliant with Factory Act Section 41(b)?
Are we compliant with Factory Act Sectio.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for MR-2023, the evidence points to a rising trend in the recorded condition parameters [1][2][3]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is fouling reducing heat-transfer effectiveness [4]. The same pattern appears on P-101, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: Are we compliant with Factory Act Section 41(b) — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. Only two independent sources support this conclusion; a third would materially raise confidence. Recommended action: Escalate to the reliability engineer for a root-cause review.

What don't we know about P-101?.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to an inspection finding that was never closed out [1][2][3]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is lubricant contamination shortening the bearing service life [4]. The same pattern appears on MECH-014, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: What don't we know about P-101 — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. Part of this evidence originates from a handwritten log, so treat the exact figures as indicative. Recommended action: Confirm the OEM threshold in the manual before acting on the trend.

answer.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to repeated corrective work against the same failure mode [1][2]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is misalignment introduced at the last coupling replacement [3][4]. The same pattern appears on MECH-014, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: P-101 ka kya haal hai — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. Part of this evidence originates from a handwritten log, so treat the exact figures as indicative. Recommended action: Verify the last recorded reading against the field instrument.

What is the status of P-101?.spoken_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to corroborating evidence across two independent documents [1][2]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is misalignment introduced at the last coupling replacement [3][4]. The same pattern appears on MECH-014, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Directly addressing the question — QUESTION: What is the status of P-101 — the records support the conclusion above but do not settle it beyond doubt. Only two independent sources support this conclusion; a third would materially raise confidence. Recommended action: Schedule a vibration survey within the next 7 days.

answer.answer_text: Based on the retrieved plant records for P-101, the evidence points to a reading approaching the OEM-stated limit [1][2]. The most consistent explanation across those sources is fouling reducing heat-transfer effectiveness [3][4]. The same pattern appears on LP-101A, which shares the service and duty cycle, so treat this as a fleet-level observation rather than a single-asset anomaly. Only two independent sources support this conclusion; a third would materially raise confidence. Recommended action: Verify the last recorded reading against the field instrument.
